coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional CDs as photoinitiators: Detection and reduction for Cr(VI)
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Nian
Zhang, Yuqi
Huang, Jiaoyan
Liu, Sicong
Qing, Taiping
Zhang, Peng
Feng, Bo
coredata.dc:description: In the realm of sensing, carbon dots (CDs) have exhibited remarkable fluorescence characteristics. In the field of catalysis, CDs can function concurrently as electron donors and acceptors and have substantial electron or hole transport capacities. In this study, both merits were integrated into one, and a one-step hydrothermal approach was used to create multifunctional CDs. On the one hand, CDs are employed as a fluorescence probe with good sensitivity and selectivity to detect Cr(VI). On the other hand, CDs exhibit strong photoreduction capabilities for Cr(VI) in the absence of any external cocatalysts or photosensitizers when acting as photoinitiators. The results show that Cr(VI) quenches the CDs fluorescence as a result of the combined effects of static quenching and the inner filter effect. At the same time, under the condition of illumination, the inner filter effect encourages the CDs electrons to move from the valence band to the conduction band, resulting in electron hole pairs. With the help of electrons' reducing power, Cr(VI) can be rapidly and effectively converted to Cr(III). The findings of this study suggest that CDs are a satisfying bi-functional carbon-based material with significant potential for the treatment of industrial wastewater.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Photoinitiator
Detection of Cr(VI)
Reduction of Cr(VI)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Cr(VI) by fluorescent probing
Analysis of optical properties of CDs
CDs for fluorescence detection of Cr(VI)
Detection mechanism of Cr(VI) by CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CDs; (b) A histogram showing the spread of particle sizes.
Fourier transform infrared spectrum of CDs (a); High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (c), N1s (d), O1s (e).

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs, and fluorescence excitation, and color development of CDs solution in sunlight and UV light (inset); (b) 3D fluorescence spectrum; (c) Fluorescence profiles of CDs under various excitation wavelengths; (d) Fluorescence profiles of CDs under various emission wavelengths.

Selectivity of CDs for different ions in the presence or absence of Cr(VI) under (a) 280 nm and (b) 370 nm excitation; Fluorescence spectra of CDs solutions with Cr(VI) concentrations ranging from 5 to 100 µM at excitation of 280 nm and 370 nm (c); Linear diagram of Cr(VI) concentration (d).

The fluorescence decay traces of CDs, CDs + Cr(VI) (a); UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, CDs + Cr(VI), and Cr(VI) (b); The Stern-Volmer curves of CDs + Cr(VI) system at excitation of 280 nm (c) and 370 nm (d).

(a) The impact of initial Cr(VI) concentration on Cr(VI) reduction; (b) Cr(VI) reduction efficiency at various CD contents; (c) A first-order kinetic curve of Cr(VI) reduction; and (d) Cr(VI) reduction rate constants.

(a) Photocatalytic reduction rates of CDs under different trapping agents; EPR signals of the TEMPO-e− (b) and DMPO-
O2 − (c) for CDs; (d) Possible photocatalytic reduction mechanism of Cr(VI).
(a) Synthesis roadmap of carbon dots CDs; (b) the detection mechanism and photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) by CDs.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional CDs as photoinitiators: Detection and reduction for Cr(VI)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environment and Resources, Xiangtan University, Xiangtan, Hunan 411105, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Environment and Resources, Xiangtan University, Xiangtan, Hunan 411105, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hunan Key Lab for Environmental Behavior of New Pollutants and Control Principle, Hunan 411105, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hunan Key Lab for Environmental Behavior of New Pollutants and Control Principle

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hunan Key Lab for Environmental Behavior of New Pollutants and Control Principle, Hunan 411105, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the realm of sensing, carbon dots (CDs) have exhibited remarkable fluorescence characteristics. In the field of catalysis, CDs can function concurrently as electron donors and acceptors and have substantial electron or hole transport capacities. In this study, both merits were integrated into one, and a one-step hydrothermal approach was used to create multifunctional CDs. On the one hand, CDs are employed as a fluorescence probe with good sensitivity and selectivity to detect Cr(VI). On the other hand, CDs exhibit strong photoreduction capabilities for Cr(VI) in the absence of any external cocatalysts or photosensitizers when acting as photoinitiators. The results show that Cr(VI) quenches the CDs fluorescence as a result of the combined effects of static quenching and the inner filter effect. At the same time, under the condition of illumination, the inner filter effect encourages the CDs electrons to move from the valence band to the conduction band, resulting in electron hole pairs. With the help of electrons' reducing power, Cr(VI) can be rapidly and effectively converted to Cr(III). The findings of this study suggest that CDs are a satisfying bi-functional carbon-based material with significant potential for the treatment of industrial wastewater.

ce:section.ce:para: Toxic heavy metals, in contrast to organic stuff, are not biodegradable and can build up in living things. Chromium (Cr), which is common in both surface water and groundwater, has become the second most harmful heavy metal contaminant among them due to its stability and persistence in the overall aquatic environment [1]. It has valence states ranging from zero (Cr(0)) to six (Cr(VI)). Nevertheless, trivalent chromium (Cr(III)) and Cr(VI) are the two main forms of Cr existing in aquatic environments, and their toxicity levels differ significantly [2,3]. For daily activities, the human body is required to absorb modest amounts of Cr(III), where adults need to take in 0.5–2 µg of Cr(III) every day [4]. However, Cr(VI) is a heavy metal pollutant that occurs mostly as oxyanions of chromate (CrO4 2−), dichromate (HCrO4 −), and dichromate (Cr2O7 2−) and has a high solubility in water. It is 100–300 times more dangerous than Cr(III) due to its facile penetration of biofilms and high oxidation potential [5]. Unfortunately, the primary cause of Cr(VI) entering environmental media and increasing its concentration above the permissible limit is anthropogenic activity. Numerous industries, such as dyeing, metal plating, the manufacturing of rust-prone steel, leather tanning, ore smelting, and others, release a significant amount of Cr(VI) into the environment [6,7]. Therefore, extensive public concern has been raised as a result of close monitoring of Cr(VI) content in water environments and the treatment of wastewater contaminated by Cr(VI) [8].

At the moment, the majority of developed methods or techniques can only detect or remove Cr(VI) alone. Such as atomic absorption spectrometry [9], the fluorescence method [10], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [11], and electrochemical detection [12] are used to detect Cr(VI). Among them, fluorescence detection is regarded as a simple, quick, economical, and dependable sensing method. In the meantime, techniques including solvent extraction [13], adsorption [14], reverse osmosis [15], photocatalytic reduction [16,17], etc. are used to clean up wastewater that contains Cr(VI) contaminants. Because of its straightforward operation, lack of pollution, and low cost, the photocatalytic reduction technique stands out among them as an appealing option for treating Cr(VI) wastewater. With the use of photocatalytic materials, which are powered by solar energy, it is possible to successfully convert highly hazardous Cr(VI) to lowly toxic Cr(III) by producing photogenerated electrons and reactive oxygen species [18]. Therefore, it is crucial that we promote the use of photocatalytic reduction in the purification of water that contains Cr(VI).

In comparison, multifunctional carbon dots (CDs) can operate as both fluorescent probes and photocatalysts. CDs have excellent fluorescence properties [19], high optical stability, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, tunable optical emission, and high water solubility [20]. On the one hand, Cr(VI) was recognized and detected using the variation in fluorescence intensity of CDs as the signal output. On the other hand, Cr(III) precipitates rapidly, is less poisonous, and has low natural mobility. Cr(VI) is converted to Cr(III) by generating electron hole pairs and superoxide radicals under light conditions using the wide optical absorption spectrum and high absorption coefficient of CDs. Numerous CDs applications have been used in various fields, including photocatalysis, bio-imaging, chemical sensing, environmental monitoring, biochemistry, and disease diagnosis [21]. Many carbon-based biosensors have been developed to date to detect and reduce Cr(VI). For instance, Gong et al. developed an “on-off-on“ fluorescent probe co-doped with N and P to detect Cr(VI) [22]. For semi-quantitative Cr(VI) field detection, Qiu et al. generated fluorescent carbon spots utilizing environmentally friendly and energy-efficient Chinese medicine [23]. Yao et al. developed iron–nitrogen-doped biochar catalysts for organic pollution removal and hexavalent chromium reduction [24]. As a consequence, it makes good sense to use the numerous functional groups present in CDs to detect and reduce Cr(VI).

Based on the previously mentioned elements, novel bifunctional CDs were created in this work using diaminobenzamide and ethanol as raw materials for the detection and reduction of Cr(VI) (Scheme 1 ). A number of virgin CDs and CDs following the addition of quenching liquid were characterized to better understand the physical and chemical properties of the CDs. Additionally, a theoretical calculation, a time-resolved fluorescence attenuation curve, and electron spin resonance analysis were used to thoroughly examine the fluorescence detection and reduction processes of CDs.

Diaminobenzamide, isopropyl alcohol (IPA), disodium ethylenediamine tetraacetate (EDTA-2Na), 5, 5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO), 2, 2, 6, 6-tetramethylpiperidinyloxy (TEMPO) and p-benzoquinone (BQ) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., LTD. Ethanol and quinine sulfate were purchased from Shanghai MacLean Biochemistry Co., Ltd. K2Cr2O7, CrCl3, CdCl2, CuSO4, FeCl2, FeCl3, KBr, Al(NO3)2·9H2O, BaCl2, NaCl, Pb(NO3)2, HgCl2, NaNO2, and KBrO3 were purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory.
Instrument characterization is found in Text S1 of Supplementary Materials.

In a 100 mL beaker, dissolve diaminobenzamide (108 mg) in ethanol (10 mL) and add 40 mL ultra-pure water. Transfer the mixture to a reactor and heat for 6 h at 160 °C. The liquid was filtered with a 0.22 µm polyethersulfone microporous membrane after cooling naturally. Next, the filtrate was collected, and usable CDs were prepared in a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cuoff value of 500 Da to eliminate ethanol and unreacted diaminobenamide and then kept in a refrigerator at 4 °C until use.
The Edinburgh Instrument's integrating sphere was used to measure absolute QY.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cr(VI) by fluorescent probing
ce:section.ce:para: For the sake of demonstrating the high selectivity of CDs for Cr(VI), a series of different metal ions and non-metal ions were detected by CDs. Under the same experimental conditions, 100 µL CDs were added to 395 µL ultra-pure water, and then 5 µL metal ions or nonmetal ions at a concentration of 10 mM were added. The fluorescence intensities of the different ions were registered at the emission wavelength of 430 nm.
See Text S2 in Supplementary Materials for the Photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction.

To obtain better optical characters for CDs, we first optimized the temperature, time, and ethanol content of the synthesis. For CDs, the highest fluorescence intensity was observed when heated at 160 °C for 6 h, as illustrated in Fig. S1. Based on this optimization, 108 mg diaminobenzenamide, 10 mL ethanol, and 40 mL ultra-pure water were heated at 160 °C for 6 h to synthesize CDs, which were then selected for further characterization and study. To start, the microscopic morphology of CDs was studied via transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and the particle size of CDs was captured by Image J software. From Fig. 1 , it can be seen that CDs exist in the form of approximately spherical nanoparticles with a main particle size distribution ranging from 1.5–3.5 nm and an average size of 2.42 ± 0.45 nm.

The various functional groups and chemical elements in CDs were characterized using FT-IR and XPS. As indicated in Fig. 2 a, the vigorous peaks at 3412 cm−1 and 3216 cm−1/1626 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectra correspond to the OH and NH bonds, respectively. The peaks at 1658 cm−1, 1400 cm−1 and 1093 cm−1 describe the extending oscillations of CO, CN, and CO, respectively, which confirm that CDs have an amino group (NH2), carbonyl (CO), and hydroxyl group (OH). XPS further verifies these results. From the XPS full spectrum, it can be seen that CDs consist of C, N, and O elements (Fig. S2). There are three peaks at 284.8, 286.2, and 287.8 eV (Fig. 2b) in the high-definition XPS spectra of C1s, corresponding to CC/CC, CN/CO, and CO groups, respectively [25]. The N1s spectrum is a good fit for pyridine N (397.5 eV), CN (399.3 eV), amino N (400.4 eV), and OCN (401.4 eV) bonds (Fig. 2c) [26,27]. The CDs in Fig. 2d have two peaks at 531.2 and 533.1 eV, corresponding to CO and CO/COH bonds, respectively [28]. It is suggested from these results that the CDs were successfully functionalized by hydroxyl, amino, and amide groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of optical properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of CDs were studied through UV–vis and steady-state fluorescence spectroscopy. The absorption bands at 280 and 320 nm in Fig. 3 a are attributed to the π-π* transition from CC within the carbon core [29], and the latter to the n-π* transition from CO/CN over the surface of CDs [30]. Aqueous CDs appear white in a sunbeam and fluoresce blue at 365 nm UV illumination (inset Fig. 3a). As shown in Fig. S3, CIE coordinates, which were acquired from fluorescence excitation data at 430 nm emission, were used to confirm the blue fluorescence of the synthesized CDs. Furthermore, the 3D fluorescence scanning pattern also showed two distinct excitation centers and two peaks in the same emission center (λex/λem = 280 nm/430 nm; λex/λem = 370 nm/430 nm) (Fig. 3b), which can be utilized for the progress of proportional fluorescent probes. Next, to investigate the fluorescence characteristics of CDs more deeply, the emission at various excitation wavelengths were trialed, illustrated in Fig. 3c. During the elevation of the excitation wavelength from 260 nm to 390 nm, the fluorescence emission of CDs at 430 nm showed an excitation wavelength-independent behavior, which may be associated with the influence of the superficial obturation function [31]. Fig. 3d exhibited excitations under various emission wavelengths, where the excitations at 280 nm and 370 nm also showed emission wavelength-independent behavior, mainly owing to their similar size and surface state, thereby not affecting the band structure of CDs [32,33]. The absolute fluorescence quantum yield of CDs was 35.5% (Fig. S4).

Fluorescence stability for fluorescent probes is an important parameter before they can be used in practical applications. Therefore, we examined the effects of NaCl at different concentrations and pH on the fluorescence degree of CDs at Ex = 280 nm and Ex = 370 nm, and recorded variations in the fluorescence degrees of CDs at 5000 s as well as 7 days. As seen from Fig. S5, CDs showed favorable fluorescence steadiness.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs for fluorescence detection of Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: To comprehensively investigate the specific selectivity of CDs for Cr(VI), fluorescence intensities of CDs with common interferin anions (Cl−, NO3 −, SO4 2−) and cations (Na+, K+, Ba2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Al3+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Cr6+) were measured at excitation wavelengths of 280 nm and 370 nm, respectively. It was obvious that only Cr(VI) significantly dampens the fluorescence of CDs at both peaks compared with other ions (Fig. 4 a and b). The sensitivity of CDs to Cr(VI) was then studied using CDs' better selectivity. The experimental results showed that the fluorescence intensity of the two excitation peaks of CDs gradually decreased with increasing Cr(VI) content, indicating that they are highly concentration-dependent. After multiple tests to calculate F370/(F280-F'280) (F280 and F370 are the fluorescence intensities at 370 nm and 280 nm before Cr(VI) was added; F'280 was the fluorescence intensity at 280 nm after the addition of Cr(VI)), a linear fit with R2 = 0.9904 was obtained. The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated at 0.589 μM according to three standard deviations of the lowest concentration detected [34], which was much less than the upper limit of effluent stipulated in China's national standards [35]. Furthermore, the performance of this proportional determination method was compared to that of other reported fluorescent probes for Cr(VI) (Table S1). As expected, our fluorescent probe showed good sensitivity and linear range. This can significantly reduce analyte-independent factor interference compared to the single-signal strategy. In order to explore the practical application of this probe, we further investigated the detection ability of CDs for Cr(VI) in real water samples. The pretreatment of water samples is shown in Text S3 of Supplementary Materials. The result showed that this probe has a good recovery rate in real water samples (Table S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection mechanism of Cr(VI) by CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs was studied according to different molecular interactions. The quenching can be divided into five categories: dynamic quenching (DQ), static quenching (SQ), foster energy resonance transfer (FRET), inner filter effect (IFE), and photoinduced electron transfer (PET) [36]. For a deeper understanding of the fluorescence quenching mechanism of Cr(VI) on CDs, the fluorescence lifetime, XPS, FT-IR, and zeta potential of CDs before and after the addition of Cr(VI) were analyzed. The fluorescence lifetime of CDs is shown in Fig. 5 a; the mean fluorescence ifetimes of CDs before and after the addition of Cr(VI) were 6.880 ns and 6.739 ns, respectively. The changes in fluorescence longevity in both dispersions were shorter than 0.5 ns, which was small enough to be ignored. Consequently, DQ or FRET is not possible [37]. From Fig. 5b, it can be seen that the UV–vis absorption peaks of Cr(VI) are evident at 274 nm as well as 372 nm. When the Cr(VI) quenching agent was added, the peak at 280 nm and concave peak at 380 nm of the CDs + Cr(VI) mixture disappeared compared to the UV–vis spectrum of CDs. The results reveal a basic duality between the absorption spectrum of Cr(VI) and the excitation spectrum of CDs, resulting in the partial absorption and shielding of CDs by Cr(VI). In addition, Cr2p peaks were deconvoluted into four main bands at 576.6, 583.1, 579.3, and 586.6 eV, which correspond to Cr(III) and Cr(VI) of Cr2p3/2 and Cr2p1/2 (Fig. S6f), respectively. This indicates that the CDs fluorescence quenching mechanism may involve electron transfer during the procedure. Therefore, IFE occurs, and Cr(VI) successfully quenches CD fluorescence [38]. The Stern–Volmer (SV) equation is available to characterize the binding force between fluorescent substances and quenchers for both static and dynamic quenches, SV equation (1) (Text S4), which can be used to describe the mechanism of FL quenching in theory for further study.

The FL intensity ratio (F0/F) was linear, as can be seen in Fig. 5c and d.
F0/F = 0.9716 + 0.0143[Cr(VI)], R2 = 0.9855; Ex = 280 nm.
F0/F = 1.0739 + 0.0107[Cr(VI)], R2 = 0.9813; Ex = 370 nm.

For τ0 = 6.739 ns and KSV = 0.0143 and 0.0107, Kq = 2.1219 × 1012 L/(mol∙s) and 1.5877 × 1012 L/(mol∙s), respectively, which are far larger than the maximal impact quenching constant (2.0 × 10)10 L/(mol∙s) [39], thus further demonstrating the SQ process. Combined with the FT-IR (Fig. S6a), it can be seen that the peak of CDs at 3216 cm−1 disappears after the addition of Cr(VI), which indicates that Cr(VI) causes the primary amine in CDs to become a secondary amine. Obviously, we can see that the 1658 cm−1 peak disappeared, indicating that the carbonyl and amino groups in the CDs participated in detecting Cr(VI). Meanwhile, the XPS picture (Fig. S6c and S6d) also showed that the CO bond vanished while the peak area of the NCO bond eased. This phenomenon indicates that Cr(VI) was reduced to trivalent chromium to some extent by CDs and that NCO at the face of the CDs can act as an active site to activate hydroxyl groups and produce O2
− to reduce Cr(VI) [40]. Cr(III) species may bind N- and O- containing groups [41]. The zeta potential of the aqueous CDs (Fig. S7) increased, easing up from −5.09 to −0.129 mV as Cr(VI) was introduced, likely due to the protonation by the combination of Cr(VI) with amino or hydroxyl groups [42]. Based on the above experimental results, we conjecture that the NH2, NCO, and OH at the faces of the CDs were involved in the reduction of negatively charged Cr(VI) to positively charged Cr(III), where the reduced Cr(III) was combined with OH and NH2 on CDs.

Given the high sensitivity of Cr(VI) detection by CDs, the possibility of reducing Cr(VI) with high toxicity onto Cr(III) was investigated further. Wastewater was prepared using potassium dichromate as a Cr(VI) source, and a 500-watt xenon lamp, simulating a solar source, was used to illuminate the mixed solution of CDs and Cr(VI). Then, every 15 minutes, the photoreducing solution was collected and tested using UV–vis spectroscopy to determine the Cr(VI) residual concentration after photoreduction. Through the use of Cr(VI) at various starting concentrations, the efficacy of Cr(VI) reduction by CDs was quantitatively assessed. As seen in Fig. 6 a, the initial concentration of Cr(VI) (C/C0) gradually decreases over time when exposed to sunlight. Additionally, the outcomes demonstrate that the proportion of CDs affects the reduction efficiency of Cr(VI) (Fig. 6b). The experimental findings indicated that the pseudo-first-order kinetics could fit the kinetic data well after analyzing the degradation rate (Fig. 6c). The photodegradation rate constant of CDs increased with the addition of more CDs, reaching its maximum value when the CDs content reached 8% (Fig. 6d). IFE can be used to explain why the degradation rate decreases when the CDs' content is greater than 8% [43]. Due to the high CDs content, which could compete for the photons required by the semiconductor, the photocatalytic activity was inhibited because few photons could reach the CDs surface. We also looked at how pH affects CDs' photocatalytic performance. As shown in Fig. S8, the reduction rate reduces with an increase in pH value. Despite the negatively charged surface of CDs, the presence of H+ aids in reducing Cr(VI) in an acidic environment to achieve sufficient reduction results. Because the OH− under alkaline conditions can effectively inhibit CrO4 2− ions, the reducing capacity of Cr(VI) gradually declines as pH rises [44]. To further analyze the valence state of Cr, the XPS spectrum of Cr2p after photocatalytic reduction by CDs was characterized. As shown in Fig. S9, the proportion of Cr(III) increased from 42.7% to 74.2%. The result indicated that Cr(VI) can be rapidly and effectively converted to Cr(III) after photocatalytic reduction with CDs.

Active ingredients are essential to the photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI). In most cases, the key active ingredients are superoxide radicals (
O2 −), electrons (e−), holes (h + ), and hydroxyl radicals (
OH) [45]. We performed free radical scavenging experiments to better understand the reduction mechanism. IPA, KBrO3, EDTA-2Na, and BQ were used to inhibit the production of
OH, e−, h + , and
O2 −, respectively. As seen in Fig. 7 a, the evident inhibitory effects of KBrO3 and BQ, with inhibitory efficiencies up to 32.6% and 53%, respectively, were contrasted with the promotion of the photocatalytic reduction rate by IPA and EDTA-2Na. When KBrO3 and BQ were introduced into the photocatalytic system, e− and
O2 − were collected, which inhibited the effectiveness of the Cr(VI)'s photocatalytic reduction, according to Text S5 Eqs. (2) and (3) [46]. As
OH and h + were captured when IPA and EDTA-2Na were added to the photocatalytic system, the photocatalytic reduction efficiency of Cr(VI) was increased. This was due to the fact that consuming
OH will encourage the creation of e− through Text S5 Eqs. (4), (5), (6), and (7) [47] by boosting the reduction performance of Cr(VI). DMPO and TEMPO were utilized as trapping agents, and electron spin resonance (ESR) characterization spectra were obtained to further demonstrate the existence of active chemicals in photocatalytic reduction. According to Fig. 7b and c, in the presence of darkness, the free radical
O2 − in the ESR exhibits no discernible signal, whereas in the presence of light, DMPO-e− and TEMPO-
O2 − exhibit a clear and potent characteristic signal. These findings suggest that during CDs photoreduction,
O2 − is created and e− transfer is sped up. Therefore, the primary active ingredients in the photoreduction were e− and
O2 −.

The band structure of CDs was further characterized by ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV-DRS) and valence band XPS spectra to better understand the photocatalytic mechanism. The band gap energy (Eg) and valence band value (VB) of CDs were 2.25 eV and 1.18 eV, respectively (Figs. S10a and S10b), implying that the conduction band (CB) of CDs was –1.07 eV (EVB = ECB + Eg). For additional confirmation, photocurrent response and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were performed (Figs. S10c and S10d). CDs have a high optical current signal value and a low electrochemical impedance value, indicating that they have efficient electron hole separation and excellent electron conductivity [48]. We suggest a potential mechanism for Cr(VI) reduction by CDs based on the findings of the aforementioned investigation. Initially, electron-hole (e−/h+) pairs were formed when CDs were exposed to light. Also more negative than the normal redox potential (O2/
O2 −, −0.33 eV) [49], the CDs' CB potential allowed oxygen and photogenerated electrons to interact to form
O2 −. CDs absorb the photoproperties e− and
O2 − generated by visible light and promote chemical processes to reduce Cr(VI) via intermolecular electron transfer, according to research on free radical scavenging and electrochemical properties.

In conclusion, we have created multifunctional CDs that may be utilized as fluorescent probes for accurate identification of Cr(VI). The linear ranges for Cr(VI) was 0.5–100 µM, whereas the detection limits were 0.589 µM. The probable mechanism of CDs on Cr(VI) based on SQ and IFE was revealed by comparing the UV–vis, XPS, FT-IR, and fluorescence lifetime results. Moreover, we explored the potential of CDs to catalyze the conversion of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) in the aqueous phase when exposed to sunlight and demonstrated that pure CDs can also photocatalyze the reduction of Cr(VI). According to the experimental findings, CDs can be employed as photoinitiators to absorb visible light, create free radicals, quickly speed up electron transfer, and stop the recombination of e−/h+ pairs, all of which enhance photocatalytic activity. This study provides a workable method for creating ratio fluorescence probes suited for Cr(VI), as well as a reference direction for the use of CDs in photocatalysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U22A20617), Hunan Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (2022JJ50131, 2022JJ30559, 2021JJ30664), and Aid Program for Science and Technology Innovative Research Team in Higher Educational Institutions of Hunan Province.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jiec.2023.09.033.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Chuang, Y. Jiming, Z. Longfei, J. Shanqing, Y. Ting, X. Wei-Wei, Z. Qichun, Two-dimensional layered organic hybrid selenidostannate coupled with polyaniline for high efficient photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction, Chem. Eng. J. 467 (2023) 143511.
